subject: Physics
chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
question_text: C, Si, and Ge have the same lattice structure. Why is C insulator while Si and Ge intrinsic semiconductors?
question_html: C, Si, and Ge have the same lattice structure. Why is C insulator while Si and Ge intrinsic semiconductors?
solution_text: The 4 bonding electrons of C, Si, or Ge lie, respectively, in the second, third, and fourth orbit. Hence, the energy required to take out an electron from these atoms (i.e., ionization energy E g ) will be least for Ge, followed by Si and highest for C. Hence, the number of free electrons for conduction in Ge and Si are significant but negligibly small for C.
solution_html: The 4 bonding electrons of C, Si, or Ge lie, respectively, in the second, third, and fourth orbit. Hence, the energy required to take out an electron from these atoms (i.e., ionization energy Eg) will be least for Ge, followed by Si and highest for C. Hence, the number of free electrons for conduction in Ge and Si are significant but negligibly small for C.

subject: Physics
chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
question_text: Suppose a pure Si crystal has 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 atoms m –3 . It is doped by 1 ppm concentration of pentavalent As. Calculate the number of electrons and holes. Given that n i = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 16 m –3 .
question_html: Suppose a pure Si crystal has 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 atoms m–3. It is doped by 1 ppm concentration of pentavalent As. Calculate the number of electrons and holes. Given that ni = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1016 m–3.
solution_text: 1 atom of Si doped out of 10 6 atoms (1 ppm of As) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 atoms net doped = {tex}5\times10^{28}\over 10^6{/tex} ​ = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 atoms 1 As atom creates 1e - excess So, no. of excess electron = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 = n e Since n e n h = n i 2 , The number of holes n h = (1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 16 ) 2 /(5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 ) = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9
solution_html:
1 atom of Si doped out of 106 atoms (1 ppm of As)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028atoms net doped ={tex}5\times10^{28}\over 10^6{/tex} ​ = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 atoms
1 As atom creates 1e- excess
So, no. of excess electron = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 = ne
Since nenh = ni2, The number of holes
nh= (1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1016)2/(5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022)
= 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 109

subject: Physics
chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
question_text: Can we take one slab of p-type semiconductor and physically join it to another n-type semiconductor to get p-n junction?
question_html: Can we take one slab of p-type semiconductor and physically join it to another n-type semiconductor to get p-n junction?
solution_text: No. Any slab, howsoever flat, will have roughness much larger than the inter-atomic crystal spacing (~2 to 3 {tex}\overset{o} A {/tex} ) and hence continuous contact at the atomic level will not be possible. The junction will behave as a discontinuity for the flowing charge carriers.
solution_html: No. Any slab, howsoever flat, will have roughness much larger than the inter-atomic crystal spacing (~2 to 3{tex}\overset{o} A {/tex}) and hence continuous contact at the atomic level will not be possible. The junction will behave as a discontinuity for the flowing charge carriers.

subject: Physics
chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
question_text: The V-I characteristic of a silicon diode is as shown in the figure. Calculate the resistance of the diode at I D = 15 mA and V D = -10V
question_html:
The V-I characteristic of a silicon diode is as shown in the figure. Calculate the resistance of the diode at

  1. ID = 15 mA and
  2. VD = -10V
solution_text: From the given curve, V = 0.8 volt for current 20 mA and V = 0.7 volt for current 10 mA, {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta I = (20 - 10) \mathrm { mA }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Delta I = 10 { mA } = 10 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { A }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Delta I =10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { A }{/tex} and {tex}\Delta V = (0.8 - 0.7 ) = 0.1 \mathrm { V }{/tex} Resistance of the diode is given by, {tex}R = \frac { \Delta V } { \Delta I }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow R = \frac { 0.1 } {10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow R = 10 \Omega{/tex} For V = -10V, we have {tex}I = - 1 \mu A = - 1 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } A{/tex} resistance of diode is given by, R = {tex}\frac VI{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R = \frac { -10 } {- 1 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } = 1.0 \times 10 ^ { 7 } \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. From the given curve, V = 0.8 volt for current 20 mA and V = 0.7 volt for current 10 mA, 
    {tex}\Rightarrow \Delta I = (20 - 10) \mathrm { mA }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Delta I = 10 { mA } = 10 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { A }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Delta I =10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { A }{/tex}
    and {tex}\Delta V = (0.8 - 0.7 ) = 0.1 \mathrm { V }{/tex} 
    Resistance of the diode is given by, {tex}R = \frac { \Delta V } { \Delta I }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow R = \frac { 0.1 } {10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow R = 10 \Omega{/tex}
  2. For V = -10V, we have
    {tex}I = - 1 \mu A = - 1 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } A{/tex}
     resistance of diode is given by, R = {tex}\frac VI{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R = \frac { -10 } {- 1 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } = 1.0 \times 10 ^ { 7 } \Omega{/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
question_text: In an n-type silicon, which of the following statement is true:
question_html: In an n-type silicon, which of the following statement is true:

options.text: Holes are minority carriers and pentavalent atoms are the dopants.
options.html: Holes are minority carriers and pentavalent atoms are the dopants.
options.explanation: In an n-type silicon, An n-type semiconductor is obtained by doping a semiconductor with a pentavalent impurity. The impurity so added produces free electrons. So the electrons are the majority carriers, while the holes are the minority carriers. An n-type semiconductor is obtained when pentavalent atoms, such as phosphorus, are doped in silicon atoms.

options.text: Holes are majority carriers and trivalent atoms are the dopants.
options.html: Holes are majority carriers and trivalent atoms are the dopants.

options.text: Electrons are minority carriers and pentavalent atoms are the dopants.
options.html: Electrons are minority carriers and pentavalent atoms are the dopants.

options.text: Electrons are majority carriers and trivalent atoms are the dopants.
options.html: Electrons are majority carriers and trivalent atoms are the dopants.

correct_option_text: Holes are minority carriers and pentavalent atoms are the dopants.

subject: Physics
chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
question_text: Which of the statements is true for p-type semiconductors?
question_html: Which of the statements is true for p-type semiconductors?

options.text: Holes are majority carriers and trivalent atoms are the dopants.
options.html: Holes are majority carriers and trivalent atoms are the dopants.
options.explanation: p-type semiconductor is obtained by doping Ge or Si with trivalent atoms. In p-type semiconductor holes are majority carriers and electrons are minority carriers.

options.text: Electrons are majority carriers and trivalent atoms are the dopants.
options.html: Electrons are majority carriers and trivalent atoms are the dopants.

options.text: Electrons are minority carriers and pentavalent atoms are the dopants.
options.html: Electrons are minority carriers and pentavalent atoms are the dopants.

options.text: Holes are minority carriers and pentavalent atoms are the dopants.
options.html: Holes are minority carriers and pentavalent atoms are the dopants.

correct_option_text: Holes are majority carriers and trivalent atoms are the dopants.

subject: Physics
chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
question_text: Carbon, silicon and germanium have four valence electrons each. These are characterized by valence and conduction bands separated by energy band gap respectively equal to (E g ) C , (E g ) Si and (E g)Ge . Which of the following statements is true?
question_html: Carbon, silicon and germanium have four valence electrons each. These are characterized by valence and conduction bands separated by energy band gap respectively equal to (Eg)C, (Eg)Si and (Eg)Ge. Which of the following statements is true?

options.text: (E g ) C > (E g ) Si > (E g ) Ge
options.html: (Eg)C > (Eg)Si > (Eg)Ge
options.explanation: The four bonding electrons of C, Si and Ge lie in the second, third and fourth orbit respectively. Hence energy required to take out an electron from these atoms is least for Ge, followed by Si and highest for C. The energy band gap is maximum for carbon (5.4 eV), less for silicon (1.1 eV) and least for germanium (0.7 eV) out of the given three elements.

options.text: (E g ) Si < (E g ) Ge < (E g ) C
options.html: (Eg)Si < (Eg)Ge < (Eg)C

options.text: (E g ) C < (E g ) Ge < (E g ) Si
options.html: (Eg)C < (Eg)Ge < (Eg)Si

options.text: (E g ) C = (E g ) Si = (E g ) Ge
options.html: (Eg)C = (Eg)Si = (Eg)Ge

correct_option_text: (E g ) C > (E g ) Si > (E g ) Ge

question_text: In an unbiased p-n junction, holes diffuse from the p-region to n-region because:
question_html: In an unbiased p-n junction, holes diffuse from the p-region to n-region because:

options.text: hole concentration in p-region is more as compared to n-region
options.html: hole concentration in p-region is more as compared to n-region
options.explanation: Hole concentration in p-region is more as compared to n-region. Therefore, they attract free electrons from n-region.

options.text: free electrons in the n-region attract them
options.html: free electrons in the n-region attract them

options.text: they move across the junction by the potential difference
options.html: they move across the junction by the potential difference

options.text: all of these
options.html: all of these

correct_option_text: hole concentration in p-region is more as compared to n-region

chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
question_text: When a forward bias is applied to a p-n junction, it
question_html: When a forward bias is applied to a p-n junction, it

options.text: lowers the potential barrier
options.html: lowers the potential barrier
options.explanation: When a forward bias is applied across the p-n junction, the applied voltage opposes the barrier voltage. Due to it, the potential barrier across the junction is lowered. Hence, depletion layer width reduces and the barrier height is decreased.

options.text: raises the potential barrier
options.html: raises the potential barrier

options.text: reduces the majority carrier current to zero
options.html: reduces the majority carrier current to zero

options.text: free electrons and holes move away from the juction
options.html: free electrons and holes move away from the juction

subject: Physics
chapter: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
question_text: In half wave rectification, what is the output frequency if the input frequency is 50 Hz. What is the output frequency of a full wave rectifier for the same input frequency.
question_html: In half wave rectification, what is the output frequency if the input frequency is 50 Hz. What is the output frequency of a full wave rectifier for the same input frequency.
solution_text: Input and output waveforms of half wave and full wave rectifier are shown in figure (a) and (b) respectively. During a cycle, half wave rectifier conducts once and full wave rectifier conducts twice, hence the output frequency for the half-wave rectifier will be same i.e. 50 Hz, whereas the output frequency for the full wave rectifier will be double, i.e., output frequency = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 = 100 Hz.
solution_html:
Input and output waveforms of half wave and full wave rectifier are shown in figure (a) and (b) respectively.
During a cycle, half wave rectifier conducts once and full wave rectifier conducts twice, hence the output frequency for the half-wave rectifier will be same i.e. 50 Hz, whereas the output frequency for the full wave rectifier will be double, i.e., output frequency = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 = 100 Hz.
